risks.title: Hardcoded JWT Secret and Default Password
risks.description: JWT_SECRET_KEY and DEFAULT_PASSWORD are hardcoded as C# string constants in AuthorizationConstants.cs and committed to the repository. Any party with read access to the codebase (including build agents, CI pipelines, forks) can sign their own JWT tokens and authenticate as any user.
risks.exploitation_path: Attacker reads repository → extracts JWT_SECRET_KEY → crafts JWT with admin role → authenticates to PublicApi with full admin privileges
risks.current_mitigation: None — code comment says TODO to change but key is not externalized
risks.recommended_mitigation: Externalize to environment variable or Azure Key Vault immediately. Rotate the key in all deployed environments. Invalidate all current tokens.

risks.title: Silent Email Stub — Account Recovery and Notifications Non-Functional
risks.description: EmailSender.SendEmailAsync returns Task.CompletedTask. No email is ever sent. Any feature triggering email (password reset, order confirmation, account verification) silently fails. No exception is raised, so callers have no indication of the failure.
risks.recommended_mitigation: Implement real email sending before production. Use conditional DI to register stub only in Development environment. Add logging when stub is used.

risks.title: Long-Lived JWT Tokens with No Revocation
risks.description: JWT tokens have a 7-day TTL. No refresh token mechanism, token blacklist, or session invalidation mechanism is visible. A stolen token remains valid for up to 7 days with no server-side way to revoke it.
risks.exploitation_path: Token stolen via XSS / network interception → attacker makes authenticated API calls for up to 7 days even after victim changes password
risks.recommended_mitigation: Reduce token TTL to 15-60 minutes. Implement refresh token endpoint. Add token blacklist via Redis or database.

risks.title: No Transaction Boundary in Checkout Flow
risks.description: OrderService.CreateOrderAsync reads basket, reads catalog items, and saves an Order in separate EF SaveChanges calls. If the basket is cleared in a subsequent operation (not visible in artifacts), there is a window for partial failure — order saved but basket not cleared. Could result in duplicate orders.
risks.evidence: OrderService.CreateOrderAsync has no explicit transaction scope; multiple repository calls
risks.exploitation_path: System crash or network failure between order save and basket clear → user retries checkout → second order created for same basket
risks.current_mitigation: EF Core implicit per-SaveChanges transaction provides atomicity within a single AddAsync/UpdateAsync call, but not across multiple calls
risks.recommended_mitigation: Wrap entire checkout in IDbContextTransaction. Alternatively mark basket as 'checked-out' atomically with order creation.

risks.title: God DbContext Makes Bounded Context Extraction Difficult
risks.description: CatalogContext owns 7 DbSets across 3 bounded contexts. Any future attempt to split Catalog, Basket, or Order into separate services or databases requires a complex, high-risk refactoring of the DbContext and all related EF migrations. Current state is a tech debt that grows with every schema change.
risks.evidence: CatalogContext.cs: DbSet<Basket>, DbSet<BasketItem>, DbSet<CatalogItem>, DbSet<CatalogBrand>, DbSet<CatalogType>, DbSet<Order>, DbSet<OrderItem>
risks.current_mitigation: None — single migration history for all entities
risks.recommended_mitigation: Introduce separate bounded context DbContexts (CatalogContext, BasketContext, OrderContext). Migrate to separate connection strings per context.

risks.title: Price Manipulation Risk in Add-to-Basket Flow
risks.description: BasketService.AddItemToBasket accepts a price parameter from the caller. If the Web project passes the price from the HTTP request body (not from a server-side catalog lookup), an attacker could manipulate the item price in the basket. The actual risk depends on the Web controller implementation, which is not in Layer 1 artifacts.
risks.evidence: IBasketService.AddItemToBasket signature: (string username, int catalogItemId, decimal price, int quantity) — price is a caller-provided parameter
risks.exploitation_path: Attacker intercepts add-to-basket request → modifies price to $0.01 → checks out at manipulated price
risks.current_mitigation: Unknown — Web controller source not in Layer 1; may validate price against catalog
risks.recommended_mitigation: Remove price from AddItemToBasket signature. Fetch catalog item price server-side within BasketService using IRepository<CatalogItem>.

risks.title: Basket Transfer Quantity Doubling on Login
risks.description: TransferBasketAsync calls AddItem for each anonymous basket item into the authenticated user's basket. If the user already had the same item in their basket before logging in, the quantity doubles (AddItem increments existing items). A user who added item X anonymously and already had item X in their account basket will end up with double the quantity.
risks.evidence: BasketService.TransferBasketAsync iterates anonymous basket items and calls AddItem on user basket — Basket.AddItem increments quantity if item already exists
risks.exploitation_path: User accidentally doubles basket quantities on every login if they browsed while logged out
risks.recommended_mitigation: Add merge strategy to TransferBasketAsync — replace existing item quantity instead of incrementing. Or clear anonymous basket if user already has all items.

risks.title: Admin Catalog Endpoints Authorization Unknown
risks.description: The PublicApi catalog CRUD endpoints (POST/PUT/DELETE api/catalog-items) were not present in Layer 1 source artifacts. It is unknown whether these endpoints enforce [Authorize(Roles=Administrators)]. If authorization is absent or misconfigured, any authenticated user (or anonymous user) could create, modify, or delete catalog items.
risks.evidence: BlazorAdmin calls POST/PUT/DELETE catalog-items with Bearer token. Endpoint source files not in Layer 1.
risks.exploitation_path: Any authenticated user sends POST api/catalog-items → creates catalog item without admin role check
risks.recommended_mitigation: Review all catalog mutation endpoints. Ensure [Authorize(Roles=AuthorizationConstants.Roles.ADMINISTRATORS)] is applied to all write endpoints.

risks.title: No Inventory or Stock Control
risks.description: There is no stock quantity, inventory count, or availability check in the domain model or any visible service. CatalogItem has no Quantity or StockAvailable property. Users can add unlimited quantities of any item to their basket and checkout without any stock validation.
risks.evidence: CatalogItem entity fields: Name, Description, Price, PictureUri, CatalogType, CatalogBrand — no stock quantity field
risks.exploitation_path: User adds 10000 units of a limited item to basket and checks out — no server validation prevents it
risks.current_mitigation: None — this appears to be a known limitation of the reference application scope
risks.recommended_mitigation: Add AvailableStock to CatalogItem. Add stock check in OrderService.CreateOrderAsync before creating order.

risks.title: BlazorAdmin localStorage Cache Not Invalidated on Server Changes
risks.description: CachedCatalogItemServiceDecorator stores catalog data in browser localStorage with a 1-minute TTL. Changes made by one admin session are not propagated to other open admin sessions. Admin A updates a catalog item; Admin B's session shows stale data for up to 60 seconds.
risks.evidence: CachedCatalogItemServiceDecorator: const int CACHE_SECOND = 60; localStorage key 'items'
risks.recommended_mitigation: Add server-sent events or SignalR push notification on catalog change. Or accept the 60-second eventual consistency as the design constraint.
